stories.title: Cybersecurity Firm PKI Solutions Opens New Downtown Portland Office
stories.excerpt: PKI Solutions has relocated its corporate headquarters to downtown Portland, occupying a larger space at 1000 SW Broadway. The company cited gaining new customers and users, as well as expanding its team, as factors in moving to the new space. PKI Solutions provides software consulting, training, professional services, and assessments to help ensure the security of organizations. “We are thrilled to be moving into 1000 SW Broadway”, said Mark B. Cooper, Founder and CEO of PKI Solutions. “This new space not only gives us room to grow our team for the long-term, but it also reinforces our commitment to returning tech companies to the downtown area.” PKI Solutions is a member of the Technology Association of Oregon, and the move aligns with the association’s mission to support local tech businesses. “PKI Solutions’ move to Downtown Portland is exactly the kind of investment we want to see more of across our region”, said Skip Newberry, President & CEO at the Technology Association of Oregon. The post Cybersecurity Firm PKI Solutions Opens New Downtown Portland Office appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: PKI Solutions relocated HQ to downtown Portland at 1000 SW Broadway.
Cybersecurity firm cited team expansion and new customer acquisition as drivers.
Move signals tech company confidence in Portland urban core recovery.
stories.summary: PKI Solutions, a software and cybersecurity consulting firm, has moved its headquarters to downtown Portland's 1000 SW Broadway, citing growth in team size and customer base. The relocation reflects management's commitment to the downtown area and alignment with the Technology Association of Oregon's mission to attract tech talent back to urban Portland.
stories.impact: Single corporate move has limited direct multifamily demand impact, but signals early stage of tech sector recovery and urban office-to-residential conversion opportunity in Portland downtown. Developer should monitor whether other regional tech firms follow, which would strengthen rental demand for younger professionals in downtown Portland submarkets.

stories.title: Wellpointe Plans $2B Affordable Senior Living Complex in Warner Center
stories.excerpt: Wellpointe Inc. unveiled plans for Viva L.A. at Warner Center, a four-phase, 100% affordable senior housing community at 6400 Canoga Ave. in Warner Center’s downtown district. The development represents a total estimated investment of $2 billion at the 4.71-acre Woodland Hills site that a Wellpointe property-holding affiliate acquired in 2025 from Parkview Financial. Designed by Gensler, Viva L.A. at Warner Center will comprise four high-rise towers ranging from 34 to 42 stories and will be located down the street from the planned Rams Village. The approximately 2.2-million-square-foot project, with 61,450 square feet of non-residential uses, will replace an existing commercial office building with 3,192 new affordable residential units. “Viva represents our conviction that affordable housing, paired with assisted living services as needed, can be delivered at the scale and density that California’s aging population in core urban centers actually needs,” said George Kutnerian, co-founder & CEO of Wellpointe. “Despite the overwhelming need for a new model, senior living continues to be built out rather than up – even in dense urban areas – and quality is treated as incompatible with affordability. Viva rejects that premise.” Where the apartment industry connects. Meet the owners, investors, developers, lenders, brokers, and service providers driving today’s multifamily market at Connect Apartments. Build relationships, uncover opportunities, and stay ahead of what’s next. The post Wellpointe Plans $2B Affordable Senior Living Complex in Warner Center appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Wellpointe plans $2B, 3,192-unit 100% affordable senior housing in Warner Center.
Four high-rise towers (34–42 stories) at 6400 Canoga Ave, phase delivery 2027+.
Large-scale vertical senior model targets aging population, reduces sprawl vs. traditional care communities.
stories.summary: Wellpointe is developing Viva L.A. at Warner Center, a $2 billion mixed-use project with 3,192 affordable senior housing units across four high-rise towers on a 4.71-acre Woodland Hills site. The development combines assisted living services, replacing single-story office with 2.2M SF of density-oriented senior housing and amenities.
stories.impact: Massive affordable senior housing concentration in Warner Center signals strong capital availability for age-restricted workforce and demonstrates institutional appetite for dense, mixed-income senior models in infill nodes. This competes for capital and entitlements against market-rate multifamily but may attract additional senior-focused investment to Warner Center submarket; developers should assess co-location opportunities with medical/wellness uses.

stories.title: Texas People & Companies, July 17, 2026
stories.excerpt: Logistics Property Company (LogiPropCo) signed a full-building lease totaling 1,004,000 square feet at 114 Logistics Park in Justin, Texas, with a packaging company. The company closed on land for its newest Texas development, Bear Creek Logistics Park, earlier this year, with work underway on the first phase. Phase 1 will include two buildings: 144,000 square feet in Building A and 390,000 square feet in Building B. Both buildings are expected to deliver in early 2028. STAG Industrial, a real estate investment trust focused on owning single-tenant industrial properties across the US, has hired Ryan Grove as Vice President, Asset Management in Dallas, TX. He is responsible for the overall performance of a regional portfolio of institutional quality assets in Dallas and some markets in the Midwest/Southeast. Ryan was previously the Vice President of Real Estate Development for Ryan Companies. Executive search firm RETS Associates handled the search. The Dallas office market continued to build momentum through the first half of 2026, posting another quarter of positive absorption as companies continued to favor newer, high-quality office space, according to JLL‘s Dallas Office Market Dynamics and Statistics, Q2 2026 report. JLL reported that the market recorded 500,551 square feet of year-to-date net absorption through the end of the second quarter, another sign that demand is gradually returning despite ongoing economic uncertainty. Endeavor Real Estate Group, in partnership with Canyon Partners Real Estate, secured financing for Lucille, a new luxury multifamily tower in Uptown Dallas located at 2700 McKinney Avenue. Lender Helaba is providing construction financing. Endeavor anticipates construction will commence later this summer, with the community expected to open its doors to tenants in the fourth quarter of 2028. The post Texas People & Companies, July 17, 2026 appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: LogiPropCo securing 1M SF industrial lease at Justin, TX; Phase 1 delivery Q1 2028.
Endeavor/Canyon Partners securing Helaba construction financing for Lucille luxury tower at 2700 McKinney, Uptown Dallas.
Dallas office posted 500K SF net absorption YTD H1 2026; positive momentum in newer, quality space.
stories.summary: Dallas industrial market remains active with LogiPropCo's two-building, 534K SF Phase 1 at Bear Creek Logistics Park completing in Q1 2028. In multifamily, Endeavor/Canyon Partners secured construction financing from Helaba for Lucille, a luxury tower in Uptown Dallas, with construction expected mid-summer 2026. Office market posted 500K SF YTD net absorption through Q2 2026, signaling gradual demand recovery.
stories.impact: Dallas office positive absorption and continued industrial strength indicate healthy labor market and corporate investment; office recovery may slow near-term multifamily supply if landlords hold on conversions, but sustained job growth supports rental demand. Helaba's construction financing for Lucille signals foreign capital confidence in Dallas luxury rents; developers should position for continued strong rental rate growth in Uptown, Cherry Creek, and DFW core markets.

stories.title: REBNY Slams Rental Ripoff Report as Painting Industry with “Broad Brush”
stories.excerpt: Mayor Zohran Mamdani on Thursday issued the Rental Ripoff Report, a package of 23 tenant protection recommendations. The Real Estate Board of New York (REBNY) responded with a statement from President James Whelan saying that the administration “continues to paint property owners with a broad brush, unfairly characterizing an entire industry.” The recommendations, derived from public hearings the Mamdani administration held citywide earlier this year, include: recognizing tenant unions, expanding tenants’ ability to organize and bargain collectively over building conditions and other shared concerns; requiring landlords to disclose when rental listings have been altered using artificial intelligence or other digital tools; modernizing building owner registration and communication systems; and using the city’s full enforcement authority to crack down on “repeat-offender” landlords through inspections, enforcement programs and litigation. “At Rental Ripoff Hearings across the five boroughs, we heard from thousands of New Yorkers living with mold that was never treated, pests that were never addressed and fees that were never explained,” Mamdani said Thursday. However, Whelan pointed out, “many of the reported conditions are concentrated in heavily rent-stabilized buildings that are facing significant operational and financial challenges.” A REBNY study released in February reported that although apartment buildings with 75% to 100% rent-stabilized units make up just 2% of the city’s housing stock, they account for 57% of buildings with at least 10 Class C violations. If you’re reading about the multifamily industry, you should be in the room with its leaders. Connect Apartments brings together the executives featured in Connect CRE’s coverage for an afternoon of high-level insights and unmatched networking on September 22 at The Biltmore in LA. The post REBNY Slams Rental Ripoff Report as Painting Industry wi
bullets: NYC Mayor Mamdani issued 23 tenant protections; REBNY counters it paints industry unfairly.
Recommendations include tenant union recognition, AI disclosure on listings, repeat-offender enforcement.
REBNY notes 57% of Class C violations in 2% of stock: heavily rent-stabilized buildings.
stories.summary: NYC Mayor Zohran Mamdani released the Rental Ripoff Report recommending 23 tenant protections, including tenant union organizing rights, AI disclosure on rental listings, and stricter enforcement on repeat-offender landlords. REBNY countered that rent-stabilized buildings—comprising only 2% of stock—account for 57% of Class C violations, arguing regulation unfairly targets the broader industry.
stories.impact: Tenant organizing and disclosure mandates will increase compliance costs and reduce pricing flexibility for market-rate operators; however, stabilized-building concentration of violations creates a bifurcated regulatory environment favoring newer, well-capitalized market-rate development. Developers should expect higher tenant screening, insurance, and legal costs in NYC; stabilized portfolios face accelerated obsolescence, opening acquisition windows for market-rate conversions and new ground-up builds in high-demand submarkets.

stories.title: Phoenix-Area Tribe Inks Deal with Industrial Developer for 1M-SF Warehouse Facility
stories.excerpt: Majestic Realty Co. and the Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community (SRPMIC) have agreed to a 99-year ground lease that will serve as the foundation for a new, light industrial project serving the Community and the greater Phoenix metro area. With an anticipated groundbreaking in 2027, the development is expected to feature up to eight Class-A light industrial buildings totaling just under one million square feet. With building sizes up to 200,000 square feet and divisible to approximately 20,000 square feet, each building will feature dock-high doors and the latest amenities and safety features found in today’s best-in-class light industrial product. The site is adjacent to the Talking Stick Resort (shown). Approved uses include granting tenant use for food processing, bottling plants, printing and publishing, machine shop, manufacturing and distribution, among others. The post Phoenix-Area Tribe Inks Deal with Industrial Developer for 1M-SF Warehouse Facility appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Majestic Realty and Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community signed 99-year ground lease for 1M SF light industrial.
Eight Class-A buildings (20K–200K SF divisible) on SRPMIC land near Talking Stick Resort.
Groundbreaking 2027; uses include food, bottling, printing, manufacturing, distribution.
stories.summary: Majestic Realty Co. and the Salt River Pima-Maricopa Indian Community agreed to a 99-year ground lease for a light industrial park of up to eight Class-A buildings totaling ~1M SF, with anticipated groundbreaking in 2027. The project, adjacent to Talking Stick Resort, will serve food processing, manufacturing, bottling, and distribution tenants across the Phoenix metro area.
stories.impact: Ground lease structure on tribal land provides long-term industrial supply certainty and may offer tax advantages for anchor tenants; development signals continued Phoenix industrial demand and population growth. Limited direct multifamily impact unless industrial employment drives residential in-migration to Phoenix exurbs; monitor whether industrial success attracts worker housing demand in Gateway/Apache Junction/Buckeye submarkets.

stories.title: Tishman Speyer Hires Former Prologis Exec Joseph Ghazal to Lead International Growth
stories.excerpt: Tishman Speyer has hired former Prologis executive Joseph Ghazal as its new senior managing director and global head of growth, the firm announced Thursday. Ghazal, formerly global chief investment officer at Prologis, will lead Tishman’s international growth strategy and expand the firm’s presence into the Middle East and India by identifying acquisition and development opportunities in those markets. “This is an exceptional opportunity to build upon one of the great foundations in global real estate,” Ghazal said in a statement. “I look forward to partnering with colleagues across the globe and to leveraging my international experience to further expand Tishman Speyer’s impact and reach.” During his more than two decades at Prologis, Ghazal held several senior leadership positions across Europe, the Middle East, Asia and the Americas. In his most recent role there as global chief investment officer, he oversaw investment strategy, capital deployment and business development. A spokesperson for Prologis said the company is “grateful for Joseph’s contributions and wish him well in his next chapter.” In his new position at Tishman, Ghazal will be based in New York City and report directly to CEO Rob Speyer. He will start in August. “Over the last decade, Tishman Speyer has intentionally and successfully doubled down on our growth and diversification strategy. We have entered new international markets, launched a range of investment vehicles and created new product lines,” Speyer said in a statement. “As someone who has spent his career doing exactly this, Joseph is perfectly positioned to help spearhead our next era of growth.” Tishman has $70 billion in assets under management across 40 markets in the U.S., Europe, Asia and Latin America. With Ghazal’s help, the firm plans to expand into several new markets. But that’s not to say the firm hasn’t been busy at its headquarters in New York City. Tishman sealed $214 million of construction financing to
bullets: Tishman Speyer hired former Prologis CIO Joseph Ghazal as global head of growth.
Ghazal leads international expansion into Middle East and India.
Move signals Tishman's capital reallocation toward emerging markets and away from core U.S. infill.
stories.summary: Tishman Speyer announced the hire of Joseph Ghazal, formerly global chief investment officer at Prologis, as senior managing director and global head of growth. Ghazal will lead Tishman's expansion into Middle East and India, leveraging his two-decade track record in Europe, EMEA, and Asia.
stories.impact: Minimal direct impact on U.S. multifamily fundamentals. Ghazal's departure from Prologis—a dominant industrial REIT—to lead Tishman's international bet may signal softening U.S. industrial cap rates or capital flight to higher-return emerging markets. Developers should monitor whether tier-one capital diverts to international opportunities, potentially tightening domestic construction financing availability.

stories.title: Nuveen Green Capital Supplies $101M C-PACE Loan for Newark Studios
stories.excerpt: Lights, camera, action! Great Point Studios has sealed $101 million of construction financing to develop a film and TV production studio campus in Newark, N.J., Commercial Observer has learned. Nuveen Green Capital originated the Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy (C-PACE) loan for the Great Point Studios Newark project to be anchored by Lionsgate. The 270,000-square-foot development, which will have six soundstages and is 100 percent pre-leased, is slated for delivery in June 2027. “We are thrilled to partner with MCCP to capitalize this significant project, which marks not one, but two milestones, as the largest C-PACE transaction in New Jersey and the first in the city of Newark,” said Mike Doty, Senior Director of Originations, Nuveen Green Capital. “Through C-PACE, the sponsor will realize meaningful cost savings by financing this project at a competitive rate over a longer term, preserving capital that can be redeployed into the development itself,” Mike Doty, Senior Director of Originations at Nuveen Green Capital, said in a statement. “As C-PACE continues to expand across asset classes and use cases, it is exciting to see it applied to a project of this scale and cultural significance, one that is strategically located close to New York City and will bring jobs, production activity, and sustainable infrastructure to Newark for years to come.” CBRE negotiated the C-PACE debt with a team consisting of Tom Traynor, Tom Rugg, William Moyer, Mark Fluent, David Milestone, Anthony Jasenski and Henry Fenmore. Great Point Studios Newark marks the first purpose-built film and TV studio in New Jersey created through tax credits. It is one of five major studio projects in the works throughout New Jersey as part of the state’s increased push to attract more film projects from Hollywood through tax incentives. “This financing is an important milestone that allows us to bring a world-class, purpose-built studio to a city with tremendous potential,”
bullets: Nuveen Green Capital provided $101M C-PACE loan for Great Point Studios Newark film production campus.
270K SF, six soundstages, 100% pre-leased to Lionsgate; delivery June 2027.
Largest C-PACE transaction in NJ; first in Newark; financing captures climate/sustainability upside.
stories.summary: Great Point Studios secured $101 million in C-PACE (Commercial Property Assessed Clean Energy) construction financing from Nuveen Green Capital for a 270,000 SF film and TV production studio in Newark, N.J., anchored by Lionsgate. The project, 100% pre-leased and slated for June 2027 delivery, is the largest C-PACE transaction in New Jersey and the first in Newark.
stories.impact: C-PACE availability in Newark signals improving capital markets confidence in the city and may crowd in other mixed-use development. For multifamily developers, this indicates Newark is attracting institutional capital; monitor whether studio jobs (production crews, post-production, talent) drive near-term residential demand in adjacent neighborhoods. C-PACE financing model offers rate/term advantages; developers should evaluate C-PACE for qualified multifamily projects with energy efficiency or adaptive-reuse angles in Newark, Jersey City, and other Gateway cities.

stories.title: New York City Hotels Are Still Struggling to Regain Pre-Pandemic Numbers
stories.excerpt: The New York City hotels and hospitality sector is among the strongest in the nation, but it still hasn’t recovered to meet its pre-pandemic occupancy and revenue metrics. This is the conclusion drawn by New York State Comptroller Thomas DiNapoli in his annual study of New York City’s hotel industry, which found that while domestic visitors have surged, international visitors remain lower than their pre-pandemic benchmarks, contributing to lower pre-pandemic occupancy, employment and room rates. “The city’s hotel industry has largely recovered from the pandemic. It is doing well, but it could be doing even better if international visitors returned more quickly,” DiNapoli said in the report released this week. “Despite the challenges, we expect the sector to continue to grow, but a complete recovery requires a return of international visitors and increased employment to keep up with demand.” Of the 65 million visitors New York City hosted in 2025, 52.4 million were domestic tourists, while international visitors only accounted for 12.5 million, which was down from 2024 and roughly 93 percent of its pre-pandemic numbers, according to DiNapoli. Moreover, while the city’s 84 percent hotel occupancy rate was the highest in the nation for the third straight year, the city’s occupancy metric remained 3.5 percent below its 2019 number. Furthermore, the $333.71 average daily room rate remained lower than its pre-pandemic average adjusted for inflation. Perhaps most concerning, the New York City hotel industry today employs 45,325 men and women, which is 13 percent fewer than it employed in 2019. “The comptroller’s report confirms what the hotel industry has been saying for years: We have yet to recover from the pandemic, and we are still thousands of jobs and millions of visitors below where we were,” said Vijay Dandapani, president and CEO of the Hotel Association of New York City, a trade group that represents 250 city hotels. That said, it’s not all doom and gloom. The nu
bullets: NYC hotels still below pre-pandemic occupancy and room rates despite strong domestic visitation.
52.4M domestic vs. 12.5M international visitors in 2025; international lag vs. 2024.
Comptroller DiNapoli: sector growing but needs international recovery and labor to meet demand.
stories.summary: New York State Comptroller Thomas DiNapoli's annual hotel study found NYC's hospitality sector has largely recovered but still lags pre-pandemic occupancy, room rates, and employment. Of 65M visitors in 2025, 52.4M were domestic; international visitation fell to 12.5M, below 2024 levels, driven by continued visa/travel barriers.
stories.impact: Weak international visitation dampens Manhattan hotel RevPAR and indirectly signals softer business travel demand, which may reduce corporate relocation and short-term rental demand in core CBD submarkets. However, domestic leisure surge and pent-up urban demand support residential multifamily; developers should focus on outer-borough and secondary-market residential in Brooklyn, Queens, and outer Manhattan where domestic tourist and residential spillover creates demand. Monitor visa policy changes as upside catalyst for hotel recovery and secondary CBD office-to-residential conversions.

stories.title: Catholic Homes Secures $147M Construction Loan for Bronx Housing Development
stories.excerpt: An affiliate of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York just secured construction financing for a housing development at 484 East 178th Street in the Bronx’s Tremont neighborhood. Catholic Homes, a nonprofit dedicated to developing and managing affordable housing for families and seniors, received a $146.9 million construction loan from the New York State Housing Finance Agency to bring 251 new units to the site, according to property records. The site at the corner of East 178th Street and Washington Avenue is currently a vacant lot. Monsignor Kevin Nelan was the signatory for Catholic Homes. The organization did not immediately respond to Commercial Observer’s request for comment. Catholic Homes acquired the development site earlier this year for $17 million from another church organization, the Roman Catholic Church of Saint Simon Stock and Saint Joseph. The NYS Housing Finance Agency — which also did not immediately respond to a request for comment — was founded in 1960 to provide financing for low- and moderate-income rental multifamily housing. Catholic Homes develops and preserves properties across New York City, with the goal of providing aid to poor and vulnerable individuals and families. All told, Catholic Homes has provided New Yorkers with 481 senior housing units, 2,350 family housing units and 128 supportive housing units. The organization has several housing and supportive developments across the city, including Clinton Broome at 157 and 165 Broome Street in Manhattan’s Lower East Side, the George Hardy at 440 East 137th Street in the Bronx’s Mott Haven neighborhood, and the Carmel Apartments at 61 Carmel Court in Staten Island. Amanda Schiavo can be reached at aschiavo@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Catholic Homes secured $146.9M NYS Housing Finance Agency construction loan for Bronx housing.
251 units on 484 East 178th Street, Tremont; site acquired 2025 for $17M from church entity.
Vacant lot conversion; nonprofit financing via state agency de-risks predevelopment.
stories.summary: Catholic Homes, a nonprofit affiliate of the Roman Catholic Archdiocese of New York, secured $146.9 million in construction financing from the New York State Housing Finance Agency for 251 new affordable housing units at 484 East 178th Street in the Bronx's Tremont neighborhood. The organization acquired the vacant lot site in 2025 for $17 million.
stories.impact: Nonprofit affordable housing concentration in Tremont signals state/public capital flowing to outer-borough infill, improving transit-adjacent vacancy conversion. While nonprofit projects do not directly compete with market-rate development, strong state HFA appetite may crowd out financing for smaller market-rate operators. Developers should monitor Tremont/East 178th Street corridor for gentrification pull from new housing density and improved neighborhood amenities; potential acquisition and repositioning opportunities for aging walk-ups in expanding zones of influence.

stories.title: Mamdani Releases Action Plan to Tackle Rental Ripoff Hearing Findings
stories.excerpt: Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s administration released a report Thursday morning detailing what it gleaned from the so-called Rental Ripoff hearings and what it plans to do about some of the most pervasive issues experienced by tenants and landlords. Complaints expressed during the hearings didn’t stray far from expectations, with 16 percent of the 2,400 participants complaining of pest infestations, as well as mold, heat, hot water issues and broken elevators. But many tenants also expressed a sense of fear of retaliation from landlords and building managers for voicing grievances. Cea Weaver, director of the Mayor’s Office to Protect Tenants, briefed reporters on the findings in the report Wednesday and discussed measures to hold bad landlords accountable while exploring new options to take control of problematic buildings and portfolios. “The recommendations [in the report], some of them are administrative actions that we can take as a city,” Weaver said during the briefing. “Some of them are legislative actions that we will need to work with the City Council to make a reality. And some of them require court action or litigation. So we’re going to be rolling these out over the next few years. This is not something that we’re going to be able to implement in the report tomorrow.” One of the ways the Mamdani administration plans to tackle complaints is by requiring that inspectors at least attempt to launch an investigation on every complaint, starting in October with the upcoming building heating season. If there are multiple complaints from different apartments within the same building, they will no longer be bunched together into one complaint, but will be treated as individual potential violations, according to the report. Audience members cheer and listen as Mayor Zohran Mamdani speaks at a “Rental Ripoff” hearing at Fordham University in the Bronx in March. PHOTO: Spencer Platt/Getty Images The city will also crack down on false self-cer
bullets: NYC Mayor Mamdani released action plan addressing Rental Ripoff hearing findings; 2,400 participants.
16% complained of pests; mold, heat, hot water, elevator issues; tenants feared retaliation.
City proposing administrative + legislative measures; Office to Protect Tenants coordinating enforcement.
stories.summary: Mayor Mamdani's administration released an action plan addressing findings from citywide Rental Ripoff hearings attended by 2,400 participants. Complaints centered on pest infestations (16%), mold, inadequate heat/hot water, broken elevators, and tenant fear of retaliation. The city plans both administrative and legislative action to hold bad landlords accountable and explore takeover of problematic buildings.
stories.impact: Enforcement and possible city receivership of 'bad' buildings will increase maintenance capex and regulatory burden on aging, under-capitalized portfolios, accelerating value destruction and creating acquisition opportunities for disciplined operators. However, heightened retaliation enforcement and tenant organizing rights will raise labor/management friction and legal costs across the sector. Developers should prioritize new construction and recently renovated assets in NYC; avoid dated portfolios in vulnerable submarkets or buildings with high complaint histories.

stories.title: Miami secures $193M permit to build civic center at Freedom Park
stories.excerpt: The city of Miami’s new civic center, which will replace an aging administrative building downtown and the city’s iconic city hall in Coconut Grove, secured a $193 million construction permit, the city confirmed. The city received its permit July 1,  just over a week before construction workers topped off the eight-story, 257,000-square-foot building on July 10. Adler Development is the developer, and Moss Construction is the general contractor.  The building will be part of the 130-acre Miami Freedom Park development, which is a $1 billion project anchored by Inter Miami CF’s stadium near Miami International Airport. The project, which includes […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Miami secures $193M permit for new civic center at Freedom Park; construction topped off July 10.
Eight-story, 257K SF building replacing downtown offices + Coconut Grove City Hall; delivery spring 2028.
Freedom Park is $1B mixed-use project anchored by Inter Miami CF stadium; $20M Metrorail pedestrian bridge.
stories.summary: Miami's new civic center at Freedom Park, a $193 million project, received construction permit July 1 and topped off July 10. The 257,000 SF, eight-story building consolidates city administrative functions from downtown Miami and Coconut Grove's 1931 Art Deco City Hall. Completion targeted for spring 2028 as part of the $1 billion Miami Freedom Park mixed-use development anchored by Inter Miami CF's stadium.
stories.impact: Civic center consolidation at Freedom Park signals city's commitment to mixed-use density near airport/stadium; Metrorail connectivity will drive residential demand for workers serving both stadium and expanded civic functions. Developers should target multifamily near Freedom Park station and along Metrorail corridor; government office relocations typically anchor long-term district stability and attract service/retail, supporting rents in adjacent residential. Monitor Freedom Park master plan for additional residential/hotel phases.

stories.title: Santa Monica, elevated: Related sets new standard for SoCal luxury with lifestyle amenities, Thermador appliances
stories.excerpt: Today’s luxury buyer isn’t just looking for fancy finishes and great views; they’re expecting a residence that facilitates their lifestyle. In our latest Developer Diary, TRD visited 700 Broadway and sat down with Related California’s Peter Garofalo to learn how the developer created an exclusive luxury residential experience in one of SoCal’s most desirable submarkets. “The ideal resident for 700 Broadway is someone who wants to have it all in a wonderfully designed and luxurious setting,” said Garofalo. “Every space has been perfectly designed to keep a balance between the beachside ease and the city’s bustling energy.” With nearly 70,000 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Related California delivering 700 Broadway in Santa Monica; three-building luxury with 70K SF amenities.
Private courtyard spans ~0.75 acres; rooftop pools, sauna, speakeasy bar; beachside-meets-urban positioning.
High-touch resident experience design (natural materials, curated dining, recreation) differentiates luxury segment.
stories.summary: Related California's 700 Broadway in Santa Monica is a three-building luxury residential development with nearly 70,000 SF of curated amenity space, including a private three-quarter-acre courtyard, rooftop pool/spa complex, and an indoor speakeasy bar. The design philosophy blends beachside ease with urban energy, targeting affluent buyers seeking lifestyle integration, not just finishes.
stories.impact: 700 Broadway exemplifies institutional developer playbook for SoCal luxury: oversized experiential amenities, architectural distinction, and lifestyle curation command premium rents and appeal to downsizing boomers/tech wealth. Developers should benchmark amenity spend and resident-experience ROI in similar coastal markets (SF, LA, San Diego); undersized or commodity amenity programs will lag in competitive luxury segments. Supply in Santa Monica remains constrained; monitor whether 700 Broadway's success and pricing validates further infill luxury in West LA or incentivizes alternative markets (Culver City, Koreatown, Long Beach).

stories.title: Moratorium on waterfront project applications in downtown West Palm heads to final vote
stories.excerpt: West Palm Beach commissioners will take a final vote on pausing new project applications for high-rise waterfront developments at next week’s meeting.  The proposed ordinance would create a six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for properties south of Monroe Drive, north of Southern Boulevard, west of Flagler Drive and east of Washington Road, according to the commission agenda. Commissioners approved it on first reading at a meeting last week; a second reading vote is scheduled for July 20.  The city cited rapid transformation of that corridor of apartment and condo buildings and is hiring Zyscovich to complete a zoning […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: West Palm Beach commissioners approved first reading of six-month moratorium on high-rise waterfront applications.
Freeze applies to Flagler Drive corridor; expires Jan 20, 2027 unless extended; city commissioning zoning analysis.
Over a dozen luxury condo projects in works totaling 2K+ units; mostly Miami-based developers (Related, Terra, BH Group).
stories.summary: West Palm Beach commissioners passed first reading of a proposed six-month moratorium on new planned development applications for high-rise waterfront properties along the Flagler Drive corridor, with second reading vote scheduled July 20. The pause reflects rapid corridor transformation and will expire January 20, 2027 unless extended. City has hired Zyscovich for zoning and economic analysis; over a dozen luxury condo projects totaling 2,000+ units are underway, many by Miami-based developers including Related Group.
stories.impact: Moratorium signals regulatory pushback on overdevelopment and will slow new waterfront condo pipeline through Q4 2026; however, projects already approved or in pre-app will likely accelerate entitlements before Jan 20 expiry. Capital will redirect to approved projects and nearby unseeded areas (i.e., 5th Avenue, Clematis Street). Developers should expect tighter design/height requirements in redrawn zoning; opportunity for mixed-income or multifamily (rental) to position as 'sustainable infill' alternative to luxury condos. Monitor post-moratorium zoning outcomes; West Palm market may favor rental over condo, reducing competitive oversupply risk for smart multifamily operators.

stories.title: MidPen floats 110-unit affordable housing project on former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale
stories.excerpt: Developer interest in downtown Sunnyvale continues with another multifamily project proposal as more tech and artificial intelligence workers flock to the Silicon Valley city.  Foster City-based nonprofit MidPen Housing is planning a 110-unit affordable housing building at 295 South Mathilda Avenue in Sunnyvale, the East Bay Times reported. All of the units, save for a market-rate manager’s unit, will be income restricted.  The development was proposed at a former Wells Fargo bank site, which would give way to the residential building. The development earned support from some residents and community groups who claim it “will assist the city by better […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: MidPen Housing floating 110-unit affordable project at 295 South Mathilda, Sunnyvale; all but manager unit income-restricted.
25% designated for previously unhoused; former Wells Fargo bank site infill; strong local community support.
AI/tech worker inflow driving multifamily investment in Silicon Valley; Beam Reach proposing 265-unit conversion at 215 Bordeaux (ex-Google offices).
stories.summary: MidPen Housing is proposing a 110-unit affordable housing project on a former Wells Fargo site in Sunnyvale, with 25% of units reserved for previously unhoused individuals. Community groups and residents support the project as smart infill utilization of vacant commercial space. Concurrently, Dallas-based Beam Reach proposed converting ex-Google offices at 215 Bordeaux into a 265-unit residential building, signaling strong multifamily demand fueled by AI industry growth.
stories.impact: Sunnyvale emerging as satellite multifamily hub as AI boom drives worker relocation from San Francisco; office-to-residential conversions and infill on underutilized commercial sites will supply affordable and market-rate units without competing for scarce residential sites. Developers should prioritize Silicon Valley second-tier cities (Sunnyvale, Mountain View, Cupertino) for near-term delivery; rents will remain strong as SF supply constraints and high costs push young tech workers south. Affordable projects anchored by mission-driven nonprofits (MidPen) signal deep public/quasi-public capital; monitor whether state/regional housing tax credits and New Markets Tax Credit flows support additional supply in Santa Clara/San Mateo counties.

stories.title: Pair of Frisco entrepreneurs ordered to pay $400M in damages to Georgetown business over alleged fraud scheme
stories.excerpt: Frisco entrepreneurs Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder were ordered to pay $400 million in damages to a Georgetown-based business after being found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury.  The pair concocted promotional materials for a luxury development called “The District” that they claimed was going to be built around the San Gabriel River. Krishnan and Gounder were sold the land by the company that sued them, Energy Commissioning Incorporated, owned by Marshall Hussain.  Energy Commissioning was subsequently hired by the pair for $21 million to build necessary infrastructure for a 400-acre, luxury waterfront development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Frisco entrepreneurs Krishnan and Gounder ordered to pay $400M in fraud damages to Georgetown business owner.
Pair fabricated 'The District' luxury waterfront development; defrauded Energy Commissioning for $21M infrastructure contract.
Scheme involved dual marketing (symbolic European investor plan + larger plan); SEC had previously targeted pair in Ponzi case.
stories.summary: Gopala Krishnan and Sakthivel Gounder, Frisco-based entrepreneurs, were found liable for breach of contract and fraud by a Williamson County jury and ordered to pay $400 million in damages to Energy Commissioning Inc., which they defrauded into a $21 million infrastructure contract for a nonexistent 400-acre luxury waterfront 'District' development. The pair had been previously targeted by the SEC in a 2023 Ponzi scheme investigation.
stories.impact: Fraud case has minimal impact on broad multifamily development fundamentals but serves as caution on speculative waterfront mixed-use projects backed by unproven sponsors in emerging markets (Georgetown, Austin periphery). Developers should conduct deeper sponsor due diligence, especially for larger ground-lease or infrastructure-dependent deals; lenders will likely tighten sponsor vetting. No direct supply/demand signal, but case illustrates regulatory and reputational risks of overpromising luxury resort/mixed-use in secondary/tertiary Texas markets.

stories.title: Bellhops and bass fishing: Wildhaven developer bets big on nature-centric luxury in Denton
stories.excerpt: A planned luxury resort might be coming to Denton, complete with six miles of horse trails to help bring residents and visitors closer to nature.  Sarasota, Florida-based An Ki Land Development’s managing director, Logan Breen, told the Dallas Business Journal that the new resort they plan on building is intended to combine upscale homes and hotels with “authentic outdoor experiences,” with a total value between $350 and $450 million when completed. The resort, called Wildhaven, is set to span 740 acres on the north side of Denton. 152 homes are set to be built in the resort, ranging from $1.5 […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: An Ki Land Development planning Wildhaven: 740-acre luxury resort in Denton with $350–450M total value.
152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), 100-key hotel, equestrian, spa, bass pond; 400 acres developed, 300+ preserved.
Noble Classic Homes building first phase (20 lots); Denton positioned as flagship for Wildhaven-branded resorts (Colorado, Florida expansion planned).
stories.summary: Sarasota-based An Ki Land Development is planning Wildhaven, a 740-acre luxury resort-style community in Denton with 152 homes ($1.5M–$3M+), a 100-key hotel, equestrian facilities, and recreational amenities. The $350–450 million project will preserve over 300 acres of natural land and will serve as the first of planned Wildhaven-branded resorts. Groundbreaking timing and exact phasing not specified; Noble Classic Homes tapped for initial home builds.
stories.impact: Wildhaven signals premium market appetite for nature-centric, lifestyle-integrated luxury in Denton as DFW's fastest-growing outer market; high land cost and resort model limit direct multifamily supply but may attract affluent households, potentially reducing multifamily absorption in closer-in DFW towns (Frisco, Plano, McKinney). However, large lot sizes (1+ acres) and resort positioning suggest different buyer profile (retiree, executive retreat) vs. primary-residence workforce housing. Developers should track Wildhaven leasing/pricing and visitor demand to gauge whether rural luxury dampens apartment demand or serves complementary niche; overall bullish on DFW population growth supporting both formats.
